name: agilon health, inc.

profile.description: agilon health, inc. operates a healthcare platform that empowers community-based physicians to transition to a total-care, value-based delivery model for senior patients. Through long-term partnerships with primary care physician groups, agilon assumes global risk capitation contracts with Medicare Advantage payers and ACO models, providing technology, analytics, capital investment, and clinical program support. Founded in 2016, headquartered in Long Beach, California, the company serves approximately 536,000 members across 26 markets.

innovation.label: WEAK
innovation.analysis: Track Record (1/3): agilon expanded from 7 to 26 markets and grew revenue from under $1B to over $6B between 2021 and 2024, but the core Total Care Model has remained fundamentally unchanged since founding — no major product pivots or new business model launches in the last five years [1][2]. The company is now contracting, having exited structurally unprofitable contracts and reduced membership 11% to 536,000 [3]. AI Deployment (0/2): No AI-powered products shipping to customers or quantified AI-driven cost savings reported. Management references 'investments in data and technology' and 'enhanced data capabilities' but without specific AI products, revenue attribution, or efficiency metrics [3][5]. Market Share (1/2): agilon is a meaningful player in value-based primary care for Medicare seniors with 536,000 members and $5.7B+ in managed revenue, but membership is declining and the company is not gaining share — it is deliberately shrinking to focus on profitability [3]. Product Pipeline (0/2): Expanded clinical pathways including heart failure programs and early disease detection initiatives, but no specific next-generation products announced with timelines or revenue visibility [3]. R&D Intensity (0/1): Capital expenditures of $13.2M on $5.9B revenue (~0.2%) is well below healthcare industry averages, reflecting the company's asset-light platform model [4].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): agilon's mission — 'to be the trusted, long-term partner of community-based physicians as they reimagine the care delivery experience for older adults' — is clear, identifies both the beneficiary (seniors) and the mechanism (physician empowerment), and differentiates from generic healthcare companies [6]. However, it hasn't been proven across decades or leadership transitions; the company is only 10 years old and undergoing its second CEO transition. Community Impact (1/2): agilon released an inaugural ESG report with its 'Total Care, Healthier Communities' impact strategy, highlighting community health outcomes in its 26 markets, but no specific dollar amounts for charitable giving or distinctive foundation programs were cited [7]. Employee Retention (1/2): agilon health is Great Place to Work certified, and employees report positive team culture and responsiveness to feedback [8]. However, the CEO transition — Steven Sell departing in August 2025, Ronald Williams serving as interim Executive Chairman, and Tim O'Rourke appointed CEO in May 2026 — signals leadership instability that undercuts trust [5]. Insider Buying (1/2): Chief Markets Officer Benjamin Shaker made a meaningful open-market purchase of 500,000 shares (pre-split) at $0.60/share in March 2026, signaling insider conviction during the turnaround period, but this was a single executive rather than broad-based insider buying [9].

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics with Forward P/E and Forward PEG excluded (company is unprofitable on a TTM basis). Balance sheet is a clear strength: D/E of 11.4% with $303M cash against only $32M total debt provides substantial financial flexibility for the turnaround [3][4]. Five-year revenue CAGR of 37.2% reflects explosive historical growth from the 2021 IPO base, though this is decelerating sharply — FY2025 revenue declined 2% to $5.93B and Q1 2026 fell 7.2% YoY to $1.42B as unprofitable contracts were exited [1][3]. TTM ROE of -109.6% and TTM profit margin of -6.1% reflect FY2025's $296M adjusted EBITDA loss, though Q1 2026 showed material improvement with $49M net income and $54M adjusted EBITDA (up 157% YoY) [3]. The trajectory is improving but TTM metrics remain deeply negative.

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (6/9): agilon's core value proposition — long-term physician partnerships with global risk capitation contracts under Medicare Advantage — is protected by deep regulatory complexity (CMS compliance, risk adjustment, quality reporting), sticky multi-year physician group relationships, and the irreplaceable nature of the physician-patient care delivery itself [2][6]. AI cannot substitute for physical examinations, chronic disease management, or the trust-based physician relationships that are agilon's competitive moat. However, agilon's platform layer — data analytics, care coordination technology, and population health management tools — faces competitive exposure from AI-native entrants and large payer/provider systems deploying their own AI-driven care management platforms. The company's technology capabilities were not differentiated enough to prevent the medical cost overruns that drove FY2025's $296M EBITDA loss [1]. AI Opportunity (0/1): No quantifiable AI-generated revenue reported. Management references 'enhanced data capabilities' and 'investments in data and technology' as contributors to Q1 2026's profitability improvement, but no specific AI products, features, or revenue attribution have been disclosed [3][5].

infinite_game.summary: agilon health operates with a genuine healthcare mission but lacks the organizational maturity and leadership continuity to score as an infinite game player. Founded in 2016 and public since 2021, the company has not yet proven its purpose through adversity or multi-decade consistency. The CEO transition — from founder Steven Sell to interim Executive Chairman Ronald Williams to new CEO Tim O'Rourke in just 10 months — reveals a company in survival mode, not one playing an infinite game. The decision to exit unprofitable contracts shows pragmatic discipline, but the underlying failures (medical cost overruns, unprofitable growth) suggest the company previously prioritized scale over sustainability.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): agilon's purpose — empowering community physicians to transform care for seniors — connects the company's work to a meaningful healthcare outcome and differentiates from pure-play insurance or hospital models [6]. However, this cause has not been tested through multi-decade execution; the company is only 10 years old and has spent the last two years unwinding the consequences of growth-at-all-costs strategy. The cause reads as genuine but is not yet validated by sustained execution through adversity. Sinek would note that a company exiting unprofitable contracts it should never have signed was playing a finite growth game, not advancing a Just Cause.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): agilon is Great Place to Work certified and employees describe a collaborative, responsive culture [8]. However, the CEO transition — Sell stepping down, Williams serving as interim, O'Rourke appointed as third leader in 10 months — creates organizational instability that undermines trust at the leadership level [5]. The rapid executive turnover, combined with the exit of unprofitable contracts and 11% membership decline, suggests an organization navigating turbulence rather than operating from a circle of safety.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): No evidence of agilon's leadership acknowledging competitors' strengths or framing competition as motivation for self-improvement. Competitive language focuses on 'transforming healthcare' and being 'the partner of choice' — positioning that emphasizes winning physician partnerships rather than learning from rivals like Oak Street Health (CVS), ChenMed, or Optum's value-based care platforms [6].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): agilon's strategic pivot from growth-at-all-costs to profitability-first represents meaningful adaptation — the company deliberately exited unprofitable contracts, reduced membership 11%, trimmed Medicare Part D exposure below 15%, and restructured contracting to improve unit economics [3][5]. This required sacrifice (revenue declining from $6.06B to an expected $5.7B) in service of long-term viability. However, this pivot was driven by financial necessity (FY2025 EBITDA loss of $296M) rather than proactive strategic vision — making it incremental adaptation rather than transformative flexibility.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): The decision to exit structurally unprofitable contracts and accept an 11% membership decline demonstrates willingness to sacrifice near-term revenue for long-term sustainability [3][5]. Ronald Williams, as Executive Chairman, made the difficult call to prioritize profitability over growth narrative — a meaningful act given that the market had punished the stock severely (pre-split price under $1.00). However, these decisions came after the company had already lost hundreds of millions — the courage was reactive (fixing mistakes) rather than proactive (investing in the future against short-term pressure).

investment_thesis: agilon health's Q1 2026 results mark a potential inflection point in the company's turnaround. Revenue of $1.42B beat estimates by 3.2%, medical margin surged 16.4% to $149M, adjusted EBITDA swung to $54M (up 157% YoY), and net income turned positive at $49M versus $12M a year ago [3]. Management raised full-year 2026 guidance to $5.74B revenue and $25M EBITDA at the midpoint, signaling confidence that the exit of unprofitable contracts and improved medical cost management are producing durable results [3]. The balance sheet provides a safety net with $303M cash against just $32M debt, and the 0.3x price-to-sales ratio implies the market prices in significant execution risk [4]. New CEO Tim O'Rourke brings 25+ years of payor and value-based care expertise, and the appointment of a permanent leader after 10 months of transition provides needed stability [5].
thesis_continued: The bear case is substantial. agilon lost $296M in adjusted EBITDA in FY2025 after medical costs spiraled out of control, and the turnaround requires sustained execution on contracting discipline, clinical program effectiveness, and medical cost management — none of which has been proven over multiple quarters yet [1]. Revenue is declining (FY2025 -2%, Q1 2026 -7.2% YoY) as unprofitable contracts roll off, and the guided $25M full-year EBITDA on $5.7B revenue implies razor-thin margins of ~0.4% even in a 'recovery' year [3]. The stock required a 1-for-25 reverse stock split to maintain NYSE compliance, reflecting the severity of the prior decline [10]. Leadership instability — three leaders in 10 months — creates execution risk during a critical turnaround window. The value-based care model faces structural challenges including unpredictable Medicare Advantage rate changes, physician group retention risk, and competition from well-capitalized entrants like CVS/Oak Street Health and Optum.
key_catalysts: Q2-Q4 2026 earnings demonstrating sustained medical margin improvement above $350M full-year guidance, validating that contract exits and clinical programs are producing durable unit economics
New CEO Tim O'Rourke establishing strategic credibility with investors through a clear multi-year plan for returning to membership growth once profitability is established
Expansion of ACO REACH and traditional Medicare models (currently 110,000 beneficiaries) as a diversification lever beyond Medicare Advantage dependence
key_risks: FY2025's $296M EBITDA loss demonstrates the fragility of the global risk capitation model — a single year of elevated medical costs or adverse coding adjustments can produce catastrophic losses on thin margins
Leadership instability with three CEOs in 10 months (Sell, Williams interim, O'Rourke) during a critical turnaround creates execution risk and potential strategic inconsistency
Declining membership (536K, down 11% YoY) and revenue ($5.7B guided, down from $6.06B in FY2024) could accelerate if physician partners lose confidence in the platform during the transition period
